minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature of the testamentary act, the nature and extent of their property, and the persons who are the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (Ala. Code § 43-8-131)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (Ala. Code § 43-8-134)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Ala. Code § 43-8-132)

electronic_wills.notes: Alabama does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Alabama

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Alabama. Alabama repealed its nuncupative will statute

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in their presence

special_provisions: Alabama is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. Alabama adopted provisions based on the Uniform Probate Code
statute_citations: Ala. Code § 43-8-130 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will under Alaska's UPC framework

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, meaning they understand the nature and extent of their property, know the natural objects of their bounty, and understand the disposition they are making
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten. Alaska SB 90 introduced electronic will provisions allowing wills in electronic form

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be competent; generally any person who can observe and attest to the signing
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing either the signing of the will or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will. Alaska follows the UPC approach

notarization.notes: Not required for validity but recommended for a self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses must sign an affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths. The will may be simultaneously executed, attested, and made self-proving (AS 13.12.504)

electronic_wills.notes: Alaska SB 90 (32nd Legislature) introduced provisions for electronic wills. An electronic will must be in an electronic record readable as text, signed electronically by the testator, and witnessed. Electronic presence of witnesses is permitted

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions of the document are in the testator's handwriting. The signature may appear anywhere on the document. Intent may be established by extrinsic evidence (AS 13.12.502(b))

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Alaska

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, deleting, or destroying)
Another individual may perform the revocatory act in the testator's conscious physical or electronic presence and by the testator's direction
revocation.notes: AS 13.12.507. Revocation of a will in its entirety revokes all codicils to that will

special_provisions: Alaska is a separate property state but adopted an opt-in community property system via the Alaska Community Property Act (AS 34.77). Surviving spouse has an elective share right of up to one-third of the augmented estate under AS 13.12.202. Alaska also has a unique self-settled spendthrift trust (Alaska Trust Act)
statute_citations: AS 13.12.507 (Revocation by writing or by act)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (ARS 14-2501)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: able to understand the extent of their property, know the individuals who would naturally benefit from their death, and understand that executing a will gives specific instructions on beneficiaries and property distribution
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten. Electronic wills are also permitted under ARS 14-2518

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be at least 18 years old and of sound mind. Arizona allows interested witnesses (beneficiaries under the will)
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing either the signing of the will or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: Arizona allows interested witnesses. A will is not invalid because it is signed by an interested witness

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Required for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses must sign an affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths (notary public), who stamps the affidavit with their official seal. The affidavit should be part of or attached to the will (ARS 14-2504)

electronic_wills.notes: Arizona recognizes electronic wills under ARS 14-2518. Requirements: readable as text at time of signing; electronic signature of testator; electronic signatures of at least two witnesses (physically or electronically present); must state date of each signature; must contain copy of testator's government-issued ID; must designate a qualified custodian (ARS 14-2520) who is not related to testator or a devisee

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material provisions are in the testator's handwriting. Testamentary intent can be established by extrinsic evidence including non-handwritten portions (ARS 14-2503)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Arizona

methods: Executing a subsequent will or electronic will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying)
Another person may perform the act in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction
revocation.notes: ARS 14-2507. An electronic will may also be revoked by deleting, canceling, or destroying the electronic record

special_provisions: Arizona is a community property state. Surviving spouse is entitled to their half of community property. Separate property may be disposed of freely by will. Arizona has no elective share statute because community property protections serve that function
statute_citations: ARS 14-2507 (Revocation by writing or by act)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound and disposing mind and memory
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of both witnesses, and both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (Ark. Code § 28-25-103)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will but may create a presumption of undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Ark. Code § 28-25-106)

electronic_wills.notes: Arkansas does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if the entire will is in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required (Ark. Code § 28-25-104)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Arkansas

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Arkansas is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has dower and curtesy rights: life estate in one-third of real property. Arkansas also provides a statutory allowance and homestead rights to the surviving spouse
statute_citations: Ark. Code § 28-25-101 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors and members of the armed forces may make a will. A conservator may also make a will on behalf of the conservatee under court supervision (Prob. Code 6100(b))

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature of the testamentary act, understand and recollect the nature and situation of their property, and remember and understand their relations to living descendants, spouse, and parents (Prob. Code 6100.5)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten (holographic). Electronic-only wills are not currently recognized

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction. A conservator may also sign under court authorization

witness_requirements.qualifications: Any generally competent person may serve as a witness. Interested witnesses do not invalidate the will but create a rebuttable presumption of undue influence for devises to them
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Both witnesses must be present at the same time and must witness either the signing of the will or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will. Witnesses must understand the instrument is the testator's will. Witnesses must sign during the testator's lifetime
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: Interested witnesses create a presumption of duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence for the devise to that witness, unless rebutted or unless at least two other disinterested witnesses signed (Prob. Code 6112)

notarization.notes: Not required. California uses an attestation clause with a statement under penalty of perjury rather than a traditional notarized self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: California uses a statement under penalty of perjury rather than a traditional notarized self-proving affidavit. The witnesses state under penalty of perjury that the testator was of legal age, appeared of sound mind, and was not under undue influence. A separate notarized affidavit is not required but can be used

electronic_wills.notes: California does not currently recognize electronic wills. PDFs, scanned documents, and digital copies are not valid. However, California's harmless error doctrine (Prob. Code 6110(c)(2)) may allow courts to validate non-compliant documents upon clear and convincing evidence of testamentary intent

holographic_wills.notes: Valid without witnesses if the signature and the material provisions are in the testator's handwriting. An undated holographic will is valid unless there is a conflict with another will or doubt about testamentary capacity during the execution period (Prob. Code 6111)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in California

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will or is inconsistent with it
Physical act of burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying the will with intent to revoke
Another person may perform the revocatory act in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction
By operation of law (dissolution of marriage revokes provisions for former spouse)
revocation.notes: Prob. Code 6120-6122. A will is also revoked by operation of law upon dissolution of marriage as to provisions benefiting the former spouse

special_provisions: California is a community property state. Each spouse owns an undivided one-half interest in community property. A testator may dispose of their half of community property and all separate property by will. No elective share statute because community property law protects the surviving spouse. California has the harmless error doctrine allowing probate of technically deficient wills upon clear and convincing evidence of testamentary intent (Prob. Code 6110(c)(2))
statute_citations: Cal. Prob. Code 6100 (Who may make a will)
Cal. Prob. Code 6110 (Witnessed wills; execution requirements)
Cal. Prob. Code 6120 (Revocation by subsequent will)
Cal. Prob. Code 6121 (Revocation by physical act)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will under the UPC framework adopted by Colorado

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, meaning they understand the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the disposition being made
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten. Colorado also recognizes electronic wills under the Colorado Uniform Electronic Wills Act (CUEWA)

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Generally competent persons. Interested witnesses do not invalidate the will. Note: Colorado uniquely allows a notarized-only will as an alternative to a witnessed will
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing either the signing of the will or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will. Witnesses may sign before or after the testator's death
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will under the UPC framework

notarization.notes: Colorado uniquely allows a will to be valid if either witnessed by two witnesses OR acknowledged before a notary public. A notarized will without witnesses is valid under CRS 15-11-502(1)(c)

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses sign an affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths. A signature affixed to a self-proving affidavit attached to a will is considered a signature affixed to the will (CRS 15-11-504)

electronic_wills.notes: Colorado adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Act (CUEWA), effective January 21, 2021 (CRS 15-11-1301 et seq.). An electronic will must be readable as text at signing, signed electronically by the testator, and witnessed by two persons in electronic presence. Electronic holographic wills are NOT permitted

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions are in the testator's handwriting. Immaterial parts may be printed, typed, or stamped. A valid holograph can be executed on a printed form if material portions are handwritten (CRS 15-11-502(2))

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Colorado

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying)
Another individual may perform the act in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction
revocation.notes: CRS 15-11-507. For electronic wills, the evidentiary standard to establish intent to revoke by physical act is clear and convincing evidence

special_provisions: Colorado is a separate property (equitable distribution) state. Provides an elective share for the surviving spouse under CRS 15-11-201 et seq. Colorado is one of the few states that allows a notarized-only will (without witnesses) as an alternative to a witnessed will
statute_citations: CRS 15-11-502 (Execution; witnessed or notarized wills; holographic wills)
CRS 15-11-507 (Revocation by writing or by act)
CRS 15-11-1301 et seq. (Colorado Uniform Electronic Wills Act)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for emancipated minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the disposition being made
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be competent witnesses; generally any person who is of sound mind
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will, but the interested witness's bequest may be reduced to the intestate share unless there are two other disinterested witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public or other authorized officer (Conn. Gen. Stat. § 45a-285)

electronic_wills.notes: Connecticut does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Connecticut

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Connecticut

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Physical act of burning, canceling, tearing, or obliterating the will by the testator or someone in the testator's presence and at their direction

special_provisions: Connecticut is a separate property state. Surviving spouse has a statutory right to elect against the will, receiving a life estate in one-third of the decedent's real and personal property. Connecticut has no forced heirship for children
statute_citations: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 45a-250 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound and disposing mind and memory
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may subscribe the testator's name at the testator's express direction and in the testator's presence (12 Del. C. § 202)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be credible witnesses at least 18 years old
witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of the witnesses, and the witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will but the bequest to the interested witness may be voided unless there are two other disinterested witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public or other authorized officer (12 Del. C. § 1305)

electronic_wills.notes: Delaware does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Delaware

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Delaware

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Delaware is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share right of one-third of the elective estate (12 Del. C. § 901). Delaware has no state estate tax for deaths after 2017
statute_citations: 12 Del. C. § 201 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No specific statutory exception for emancipated minors in the will statute

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound and disposing mind and capable of executing a valid deed or contract (DC Code 18-102)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten, but all wills must meet standard execution requirements. Electronic wills are recognized under DC Code 18-901 et seq.

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction (DC Code 18-103)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must attest and subscribe in the presence of the testator. The testator must either sign before the witnesses or declare to them that the signature is theirs and state that the document is their last will and testament (DC Code 18-103)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise or legacy to an attesting witness is void under DC Code 18-105. The interested witness may not retain or demand any such devise or legacy. This is one of the strictest interested witness rules in any jurisdiction

notarization.notes: Not required for standard wills. DC historically did not have a traditional self-proving affidavit for paper wills. Electronic wills may be made self-proving with notarization

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: For traditional paper wills, DC historically did not provide a self-proving affidavit. The Uniform Electronic Wills Amendment Act of 2022 introduced self-proving provisions for electronic wills under DC Code 18-908, requiring acknowledgment by the testator and affidavits of witnesses before an authorized officer

electronic_wills.notes: DC recognizes electronic wills under DC Code 18-901 et seq. (Uniform Electronic Wills Amendment Act of 2022, D.C. Law 24-296). An electronic will must be readable as text at signing, electronically signed by the testator, and witnessed. Electronic wills may be made self-proving under DC Code 18-908

holographic_wills.notes: DC does not recognize unwitnessed holographic wills. A handwritten will is only valid if it has two witnesses and follows all standard execution requirements under DC Code 18-103

nuncupative_wills.notes: Nuncupative wills made after January 1, 1902 are not valid except for persons in actual military or naval service or mariners at sea, who may dispose of personal property by word of mouth. The oral will must be reduced to writing within ten days of its making (DC Code 18-107)

methods: Executing a later will, codicil, or other writing declaring the revocation, executed as provided by DC Code 18-103
By implication of law
revocation.notes: DC Code 18-109. Notably, DC does not explicitly include physical destruction (burning, tearing, etc.) as a method of revocation in the statute. Revocation must generally be by a subsequent written instrument executed with the same formalities as a will, or by implication of law

special_provisions: DC is a separate property jurisdiction. Provides an elective share for the surviving spouse. DC has a unique revocation statute that appears to require a written revocation instrument rather than permitting revocation by physical destruction alone. DC's interested witness rule is strict: devises to attesting witnesses are void under DC Code 18-105. DC adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Amendment Act of 2022 (D.C. Law 24-296). Nuncupative wills permitted only for military personnel and mariners, with a 10-day writing requirement
statute_citations: DC Code 18-102 (Who may make a will)
DC Code 18-103 (Execution of written will; attestation)
D.C. Law 24-296 (Uniform Electronic Wills Amendment Act of 2022)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (Fla. Stat. § 732.501)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of the property to be disposed of, the relationship to those who would naturally claim a benefit, and the practical effect of the will
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed or printed. Florida is strict about formalities

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction. The signature must be at the end of the will (Fla. Stat. § 732.502)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of both witnesses. Both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator and in the presence of each other (Fla. Stat. § 732.502)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will. Florida repealed the interested witness penalty (Fla. Stat. § 732.504)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public or other authorized officer (Fla. Stat. § 732.503)

electronic_wills.notes: Florida adopted the Electronic Wills Act effective January 1, 2020 (Fla. Stat. § 732.521-732.528). Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, electronically signed by the testator, and witnessed by two persons who may be present by audio-video communication if a supervised by a notary

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Florida, even if valid where executed

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Florida

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical act of burning, tearing, canceling, defacing, obliterating, or destroying the will by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence
A writing executed with will formalities

special_provisions: Florida is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share of 30% of the elective estate (Fla. Stat. § 732.2065). Florida has strong homestead protections: the homestead cannot be devised away from a surviving spouse or minor children. Florida has no state income tax or estate tax
statute_citations: Fla. Stat. § 732.501 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Georgia has one of the lowest minimum ages to make a will in the nation (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-10)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must have sufficient intellect to enable them to have a decided and rational desire as to the disposition of property and to understand the nature of the testamentary act
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-20)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of the witnesses, and the witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-20)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-23)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-24)

electronic_wills.notes: Georgia enacted electronic will provisions effective July 1, 2022. Electronic wills must be created, signed, and witnessed electronically and stored by a qualified custodian

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Georgia

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last illness, in the testator's dwelling or where they resided for 10+ days (except sudden illness), before two competent witnesses, and limited to $100 unless reduced to writing within 30 days (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-27)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction by the testator or at the testator's direction
Any writing executed with the same formalities as a will

special_provisions: Georgia is a separate property/common law state. Georgia has NO elective share statute — a surviving spouse can be disinherited. However, a surviving spouse can petition for a year's support from the estate (O.C.G.A. § 53-3-1). Georgia's minimum age of 14 is the lowest in the nation. Georgia is one of the few states without elective share protections

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will under the UPC framework

writing_requirement: Must be in writing on paper. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: The will is not invalid because it is signed by an interested witness

electronic_wills.notes: Hawaii does not currently recognize electronic wills. The will must be on paper in writing

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions of the document are in the testator's handwriting. Intent may be established by extrinsic evidence, including non-handwritten portions (HRS 560:2-502)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Hawaii

Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will. Idaho follows the UPC

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and memory, meaning they understand the nature of their property, their relationship to family members, and the effect of the testamentary act

witness_requirements.qualifications: Generally competent persons. Interested witnesses do not invalidate the will

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Idaho

special_provisions: Louisiana is a community property state and uses civil law (not common law) for succession. C.C. art. 1493). The forced portion is one-quarter of the estate if one forced heir, one-half if two or more. Louisiana's succession law is rooted in French/Spanish civil law traditions and is unique among US states. Act No. 30 of 2025 relaxed some formal requirements for notarial testaments
