writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A bequest to an attesting witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses, or the interested witness would have been an heir at law

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before an authorized officer (RSA 551:2-a)

electronic_wills.notes: New Hampshire does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in New Hampshire

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: soldiers in actual military service and mariners at sea may make nuncupative wills for personal property (RSA 551:16)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or someone in the testator's presence and at their direction
Operation of law (divorce revokes dispositions to former spouse)

special_provisions: New Hampshire is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has the right to waive the will and take their intestate share. New Hampshire has no state estate or inheritance tax

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (N.J.S.A. 3B:3-1)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the disposition being made
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (N.J.S.A. 3B:3-2)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing either the signing or the testator's acknowledgment
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (N.J.S.A. 3B:3-8)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit. Required for electronic wills

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths (N.J.S.A. 3B:3-4)

electronic_wills.notes: New Jersey adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Act. Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, electronically signed by the testator, and witnessed by two persons. Must be notarized

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions are in the testator's handwriting (N.J.S.A. 3B:3-3)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in New Jersey

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: New Jersey is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share right of one-third of the augmented estate (N.J.S.A. 3B:8-1). New Jersey eliminated its estate tax effective January 1, 2018

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will under UPC provisions

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature of the testamentary act, the extent of their property, and the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten, but all wills must comply with witness requirements. Unwitnessed holographic wills are not valid if created in New Mexico

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign at the testator's express direction in the testator's direct physical presence

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator and of each other (NMSA 45-2-502). This is stricter than many UPC states
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will under the UPC framework

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Required for a self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses must sign an affidavit before a notary public or other officer authorized to administer oaths (NMSA 45-2-504)

electronic_wills.notes: New Mexico has not adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Act. Electronic wills are not recognized

holographic_wills.notes: Unwitnessed holographic wills created in New Mexico are not valid. A handwritten will is only valid if it meets all standard execution requirements including two witnesses. Foreign holographic wills may be recognized if valid where executed (NMSA 45-2-506)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in New Mexico

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying)
Another person may perform the act in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

special_provisions: New Mexico is a community property state. Each spouse owns an undivided one-half interest in community property. No elective share statute because community property protections serve that function. New Mexico requires witnesses to sign in the presence of each other (stricter than many UPC states)
statute_citations: NMSA 45-2-506 (Choice of law as to execution)
NMSA 45-2-507 (Revocation by writing or by act)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and memory: understand the nature and consequences of making a will, know the nature and extent of their property, and know the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed or printed. Holographic wills only recognized for military/mariners

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction. The testator must sign or acknowledge at the end of the will (EPTL § 3-2.1)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be competent persons at least 18 years old
witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must declare to each witness that the instrument is their will. Each witness must sign within 30 days of attestation. Witnesses must sign at the testator's request
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A bequest to an attesting witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses. The interested witness may still serve as a witness, and the rest of the will remains valid (EPTL § 3-3.2)

notarization.notes: Not required. New York uses an attestation clause rather than a self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: New York does not use a traditional self-proving affidavit. A properly drafted attestation clause creates a presumption of due execution (EPTL § 3-2.1). SCPA § 1406 allows witnesses to submit affidavits in lieu of testimony

electronic_wills.notes: New York signed an electronic wills law in 2025 (S2224/A1614), effective December 2027. Will allow electronic execution and remote witnessing via audio-video technology

holographic_wills.notes: Only for: (1) members of the armed forces during a conflict, (2) persons serving with or accompanying armed forces, or (3) mariners at sea. Such wills expire after specific time periods (EPTL § 3-2.2)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Only for the same limited classes as holographic wills: armed forces members and mariners at sea. Must be made during last sickness or in fear of imminent death (EPTL § 3-2.2)

methods: Executing a subsequent will that expressly revokes the prior will
Physical act of burning, tearing, cutting, canceling, obliterating by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in their presence
A later will revokes a prior will to the extent of inconsistency
revocation.notes: EPTL § 3-4.1. Marriage does not revoke a pre-existing will, but divorce revokes provisions for the former spouse

special_provisions: New York is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has a right of election of the greater of $50,000 or one-third of the net estate (EPTL § 5-1.1-A). New York requires the testator's signature at the end of the will — provisions after the signature are invalid. Publication requirement: testator must declare to witnesses that the instrument is their will
statute_citations: EPTL § 5-1.1-A (Right of election by surviving spouse)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (N.C.G.S. § 31-1)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may subscribe the testator's name in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.3)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two witnesses, and both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.3)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (N.C.G.S. § 31-10)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (N.C.G.S. § 31-11.6)

electronic_wills.notes: North Carolina does not currently recognize electronic wills, though NC enacted provisions for electronic storage of paper wills effective January 2026

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if written entirely in the testator's handwriting, signed by the testator, and found after death among the testator's valuable papers or effects, or in a safe-deposit box, or in the custody of some person with whom it was deposited for safekeeping (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.4)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last sickness or in imminent peril of death, before two competent witnesses. Only valid for personal property up to a limited value. Must be proved within 6 months after the speaking of the alleged testamentary words (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.5)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: North Carolina is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share ranging from a life estate in one-third to an outright share depending on the number of surviving descendants (N.C.G.S. § 30-3.1). North Carolina's holographic will must be found among valuable papers — unique requirement. North Carolina has no state estate tax

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (NDCC § 30.1-08-01)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (NDCC § 30.1-08-02)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (NDCC § 30.1-08-05)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit. A notarized will may also be self-proving

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (NDCC § 30.1-08-04)

electronic_wills.notes: North Dakota adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Act (NDCC Ch. 30.1-37). Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, electronically signed, and witnessed

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions are in the testator's handwriting (NDCC § 30.1-08-02(2))

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in North Dakota

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: North Dakota is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share under the augmented estate system (NDCC § 30.1-05-02). North Dakota adopted the Uniform Probate Code. North Dakota has no state estate tax

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and memory and not under restraint (ORC § 2107.02)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed or printed. Ohio does not recognize holographic wills

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's conscious presence and at the testator's express direction. The signature must be at the end of the will (ORC § 2107.03)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The will must be attested and subscribed by two or more competent witnesses in the presence of the testator (ORC § 2107.03)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness may serve but the bequest to that witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses (ORC § 2107.15)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Ohio does not have a self-proving affidavit statute. Witnesses must testify at probate or provide a deposition/affidavit

electronic_wills.notes: Ohio does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Ohio

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last sickness, at the testator's habitual dwelling (or where they became sick), before two disinterested witnesses. Only for personal property. Must be reduced to writing within 10 days and proved within 6 months (ORC § 2107.60)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Ohio is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share: if one or two children or their representatives survive, one-third; if no children or their representatives survive, one-half (ORC § 2106.01). Ohio is one of the few states without a self-proving affidavit statute. Ohio requires the testator's signature at the end of the will. Ohio has a state estate tax for deaths before January 1, 2013 only

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and over 18 years of age (84 Okla. Stat. § 41)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may subscribe the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The will must be subscribed in the presence of the testator by two or more competent witnesses who saw the testator subscribe or heard the testator acknowledge the will (84 Okla. Stat. § 55)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A bequest to a subscribing witness is void unless there are two other disinterested subscribing witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (58 Okla. Stat. § 71)

electronic_wills.notes: Oklahoma does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if entirely in the testator's own handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required (84 Okla. Stat. § 54)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made in the testator's last sickness or in imminent peril of death. Must be proved by two witnesses. For personal property only up to $1,000 (84 Okla. Stat. § 46)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Oklahoma is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share: forced share of one-half of property acquired during the marriage by joint industry (84 Okla. Stat. § 44). Oklahoma has no state estate or inheritance tax
statute_citations: 84 Okla. Stat. § 41 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: A person who is lawfully married or emancipated may make a will regardless of age (ORS 112.225)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature of the testamentary act, know the nature and extent of their property, and know the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten, but all wills must meet standard execution requirements including witnesses. Purely holographic (unwitnessed handwritten) wills are not valid

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (ORS 112.235)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Both witnesses must sign the will within a reasonable time. Both witnesses must sign before the death of the testator (ORS 112.235)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: Oregon does not automatically invalidate a will signed by an interested witness, but a devise to an attesting witness creates a rebuttable presumption of undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Used for self-proving affidavits

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses sign an affidavit before a notary public stating the relevant facts about execution. Allows the court to accept the will without contacting witnesses (ORS 113.055)

electronic_wills.notes: Oregon does not currently recognize electronic wills. If a will is prepared online, it must be printed, physically signed, and witnessed

holographic_wills.notes: Oregon does not recognize purely holographic (unwitnessed handwritten) wills. A handwritten will is valid only if it meets all standard execution requirements including two witnesses. Oregon provides a harmless error/substantial compliance mechanism under ORS 112.238

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Oregon except possibly for members of the armed forces while in actual military service during conflict

methods: Executing a subsequent will that expressly revokes the prior will
A subsequent will inconsistent with the prior will
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) with intent to revoke
By operation of law upon subsequent marriage of the testator (ORS 112.305, unless exceptions apply)
Another person may destroy the will in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction
revocation.notes: ORS 112.285 (express revocation), ORS 112.305 (revocation by marriage). Marriage revokes a prior will unless made in contemplation of the marriage or a prenuptial agreement exists

special_provisions: Oregon is a separate property state but adopted the Uniform Disposition of Community Property Rights at Death Act (ORS 112.705-112.775) for couples moving from community property states. Provides an elective share for the surviving spouse under ORS 114.600-114.725, based on an augmented estate concept with the share percentage varying by length of marriage. Community property is excluded from the augmented estate. Oregon has a harmless error/substantial compliance doctrine (ORS 112.238)

minimum_age.exceptions: Married minors may make a will regardless of age (20 Pa.C.S. § 2501)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: able to understand the nature of the act, know the nature and extent of their property, and know the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence. The signature must be at the end of the will (20 Pa.C.S. § 2502)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Pennsylvania does not require witnesses at execution. Two witnesses are required at probate if the will is not self-proving
witness_requirements.presence_rules: No witness presence rules at execution. At probate, two witnesses must identify the testator's signature
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: Since witnesses are not required at execution, this is generally not an issue

notarization.notes: Not required. A notarized will becomes self-proving and does not need witnesses at probate

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: A will is self-proving if the testator's signature is notarized or if it includes an attestation clause with witnesses' signatures before a notary (20 Pa.C.S. § 3132.1)

electronic_wills.notes: Pennsylvania does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Effectively recognized because Pennsylvania does not require witnesses at execution. A handwritten, signed will is valid as long as the testator's signature can be proved at probate

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Pennsylvania

methods: Executing a later will or codicil that expressly or impliedly revokes the prior will
Physical act (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Pennsylvania is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share of one-third of certain property (20 Pa.C.S. § 2203). Pennsylvania is unique in not requiring witnesses at the time of will execution — only a signature is needed. Pennsylvania has an inheritance tax (rates vary by relationship to decedent)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the disposition being made
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (R.I. Gen. Laws § 33-5-5)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A bequest to an attesting witness is not automatically void, but may raise questions about undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (R.I. Gen. Laws § 33-7-26)

electronic_wills.notes: Rhode Island does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Rhode Island

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: soldiers in actual military service and mariners at sea for personal property only (R.I. Gen. Laws § 33-5-10)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or someone in the testator's presence and at their direction

special_provisions: Rhode Island is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share right: life estate in all real property and an outright share of personal property. Rhode Island uses dower and curtesy rights which are largely outdated in most other states
statute_citations: R.I. Gen. Laws § 33-5-2 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (S.C. Code § 62-2-501)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (S.C. Code § 62-2-502)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (S.C. Code § 62-2-505)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (S.C. Code § 62-2-504)

electronic_wills.notes: South Carolina does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in South Carolina

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in South Carolina

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: South Carolina is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share of one-third of the probate estate (S.C. Code § 62-2-201). South Carolina adopted the Uniform Probate Code
statute_citations: S.C. Code § 62-2-501 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (SDCL § 29A-2-501)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (SDCL § 29A-2-502)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (SDCL § 29A-2-505)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (SDCL § 29A-2-504)

electronic_wills.notes: South Dakota does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions are in the testator's handwriting (SDCL § 29A-2-502(2))

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in South Dakota

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: South Dakota is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share under the augmented estate system (SDCL § 29A-2-202). South Dakota adopted the Uniform Probate Code. South Dakota has no state income tax, estate tax, or inheritance tax. South Dakota is known for its favorable trust laws

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (Tenn. Code § 32-1-102)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (Tenn. Code § 32-1-104)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two witnesses, and the witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (Tenn. Code § 32-1-104)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will but may create an inference of undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Tenn. Code § 32-2-110)

electronic_wills.notes: Tennessee does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution but must be proven by two witnesses to the handwriting (Tenn. Code § 32-1-105)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made in imminent peril of death, before two disinterested witnesses, and only for personal property not exceeding $1,000 in value. Must be reduced to writing within 20 days and submitted for probate within 6 months (Tenn. Code § 32-1-106)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Tennessee is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share (Tenn. Code § 31-4-101). Tennessee has no state income tax (Hall Tax repealed 2021) and no state estate tax. Tennessee allows a real property disposition separate from the will via a recorded deed
statute_citations: Tenn. Code § 32-1-102 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: A person who is or has been lawfully married, or a member of the armed forces, an auxiliary thereof, or the maritime service, may make a will regardless of age (Tex. Est. Code 251.001)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature and effect of the testamentary act, know the general nature and extent of their property, and know their next of kin and the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or entirely handwritten (holographic). Electronic wills are not currently recognized in Texas

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign on the testator's behalf in the testator's presence and under the testator's direction (Tex. Est. Code 251.051)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be credible witnesses who are at least 14 years of age
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must subscribe their names to the will in their own handwriting in the testator's presence (Tex. Est. Code 251.051)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not automatically invalidate the will. However, if an interested witness is one of only two attesting witnesses, their inheritance may be void unless corroborated by disinterested evidence (Tex. Est. Code 254.002)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Required for a self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and attesting witnesses must sign a self-proving affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths (notary), who affixes their official seal. The affidavit must be attached or annexed to the will. May be made at execution or later (Tex. Est. Code 251.104)

electronic_wills.notes: Texas does not currently recognize electronic wills. Legislative proposals have been introduced but not enacted as of 2026. A will must be physically written and signed

holographic_wills.notes: A will written wholly in the testator's own handwriting is valid without attesting witnesses. Must be entirely in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator (Tex. Est. Code 251.052)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills made after September 1, 2007 are no longer valid in Texas. Prior to that date, nuncupative wills were permitted in limited circumstances

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will or is wholly inconsistent
Destroying or canceling the will, or causing it to be destroyed or canceled in the testator's presence
revocation.notes: Tex. Est. Code 253.002. Texas does not recognize partial revocation by physical act. A self-proved will may be revoked in the same manner as a non-self-proved will

special_provisions: Texas is a community property state. Each spouse owns an undivided one-half interest in community property. No elective share statute. Witnesses must be at least 14 (younger than most states). Texas has a unique homestead protection that cannot be overridden by will if a surviving spouse or minor children exist. Nuncupative wills were abolished effective September 1, 2007
statute_citations: Tex. Est. Code 251.001 (Who may make a will)
Tex. Est. Code 251.051 (Written, signed, and attested)
Tex. Est. Code 251.052 (Exception for holographic wills)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will under the UPC framework

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the nature of the testamentary act
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten. Electronic wills are also recognized

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Generally competent persons. Interested witnesses do not invalidate the will
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing either the signing of the will or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: The will is not invalidated by the signature of an interested witness under Utah's UPC framework

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Required for a self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses must sign an affidavit before a notary public or other officer authorized to administer oaths (Utah Code 75-2-504)

electronic_wills.notes: Utah recognizes electronic wills under Utah Code 75-2-1001 et seq. An e-will must be readable as text at signing, can be stored and signed electronically, and requires the same witness requirements as a traditional will

holographic_wills.notes: Valid whether or not witnessed if the signature and material portions of the document are in the testator's handwriting (Utah Code 75-2-502(2))

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Utah

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the previous will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act on the will (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying)
Another individual may perform the act in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction
A burning, tearing, or canceling is a revocatory act whether or not it touched any of the words on the will

special_provisions: Utah is a separate property state. Provides an elective share for the surviving spouse under Utah Code 75-2-202 following the UPC augmented estate approach. Utah has adopted electronic will provisions making it one of the more progressive states for digital estate planning
statute_citations: Utah Code 75-2-501 (Who may make a will)
Utah Code 75-2-502 (Execution; witnessed wills; holographic wills)
Utah Code 75-2-507 (Revocation by writing or by act)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (14 V.S.A. § 1)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the disposition being made
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator AND in the presence of each other — Vermont is one of the few states requiring witnesses to sign in each other's presence
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A bequest to an attesting witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses to the will

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; recommended for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (14 V.S.A. § 5)

electronic_wills.notes: Vermont does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Vermont

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Vermont

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or someone in the testator's presence and at their direction

special_provisions: Vermont is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. Vermont requires witnesses to sign in each other's presence, which is stricter than most states. Vermont also recognizes civil unions and extends spousal rights to civil union partners

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (Va. Code § 64.2-401)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (Va. Code § 64.2-401)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two competent witnesses who are present at the same time. Witnesses must sign in the testator's presence (Va. Code § 64.2-403)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (Va. Code § 64.2-404)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Va. Code § 64.2-405)

electronic_wills.notes: Virginia does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution. Must be proved by two disinterested witnesses to the handwriting (Va. Code § 64.2-403)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Virginia abolished nuncupative wills effective July 1, 2007

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical act (cutting, tearing, burning, obliterating, canceling, or destroying) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Virginia is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share: if no surviving descendants, one-half of the augmented estate; if surviving descendants, one-third of the augmented estate (Va. Code § 64.2-302). Virginia requires witnesses to be present at the same time, which is stricter than some states
statute_citations: Va. Code § 64.2-401 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No specific statutory exception for emancipated minors in the will statute (RCW 11.12.010)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind: understand the nature of the act, the nature and extent of their property, and the identity of the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten, but all wills must be properly witnessed. Electronic wills are recognized under the Uniform Electronic Wills Act (effective Jan. 1, 2022)

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign under the testator's direction in the testator's presence (RCW 11.12.020)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Witnesses must subscribe their names to the will or sign an affidavit complying with RCW 11.20.020(2), in the presence of the testator and at the testator's direction or request. Electronic presence is permitted under the UEWA
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise to an attesting witness is not void, but the interested witness creates a rebuttable presumption that the devise was procured by duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence (RCW 11.12.160)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Used for self-proving affidavits

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Witnesses may sign an affidavit that complies with RCW 11.20.020(2) as an alternative to or in addition to subscribing their names to the will. The affidavit is sworn before a notary. For electronic wills, the will must remain in custody of a qualified custodian to be treated as self-proving

electronic_wills.notes: Washington adopted the Uniform Electronic Wills Act (UEWA), effective January 1, 2022 (RCW 11.12.400-11.12.491). Electronic wills must be readable as text, signed electronically by the testator, and witnessed. If not maintained by a qualified custodian, treated as a lost or destroyed will under RCW 11.20.070

holographic_wills.notes: Washington does not recognize unwitnessed holographic wills. A handwritten will is valid only if it meets all standard execution requirements including two witnesses

nuncupative_wills.notes: Valid only for members of the armed forces or persons employed on a vessel of the United States merchant marine. Limited to personal property not exceeding $1,000. Must be proved by two witnesses present at the making; the testator must have bidden someone bear witness; must be made during testator's last sickness; proof must be offered within six months (RCW 11.12.025)

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Being burnt, torn, canceled, obliterated, or destroyed with intent to revoke, by testator or by another in testator's presence and at testator's direction
revocation.notes: RCW 11.12.040. Revocation of a will in its entirety revokes its codicils unless contrary to testator's intent

special_provisions: Washington is a community property state. Each spouse owns an undivided one-half interest in community property. No elective share statute because community property law protects the surviving spouse. Washington allows community property agreements that can transfer all community property to the surviving spouse without probate. Washington was an early adopter of the UEWA for electronic wills

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and not under undue influence (W. Va. Code § 41-1-1)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two competent witnesses who are present at the same time. Witnesses must sign in the testator's presence (W. Va. Code § 41-1-3)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise to an attesting witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (W. Va. Code § 41-5-15)

electronic_wills.notes: West Virginia enacted electronic will provisions (W. Va. Code § 41-1-4a). Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, signed electronically by the testator, and witnessed

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution. Must be proved by two witnesses to the handwriting

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: only for military personnel in actual military service and mariners at sea (W. Va. Code § 41-1-5)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: West Virginia is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. West Virginia requires witnesses to be present at the same time
statute_citations: W. Va. Code § 41-1-1 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: No statutory exceptions for minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (Wis. Stat. § 853.01)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another individual may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (Wis. Stat. § 853.03)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment. Wisconsin enacted remote witnessing via attorney-supervised audiovisual communication in 2024 (2023 Act 130)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (Wis. Stat. § 853.07)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Wis. Stat. § 853.04)

electronic_wills.notes: Wisconsin does not have a full electronic wills statute but enacted remote witnessing provisions via attorney-supervised audiovisual communication in 2024 (2023 Act 130)

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Wisconsin

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Wisconsin

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: Wisconsin is a COMMUNITY PROPERTY state — the only one in the Midwest. It adopted the Uniform Marital Property Act (Wis. Stat. Ch. 766). Surviving spouse has an elective share of the augmented deferred marital property estate (Wis. Stat. § 861.02). Wisconsin enacted remote audiovisual witnessing supervised by an attorney in 2024 (2023 Act 130)
statute_citations: Wis. Stat. § 853.01 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Any person of legal age (18) and sound mind may make a will. No specific statutory exception for emancipated minors

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, meaning they have not been deemed incompetent in a prior legal proceeding and they understand the nature and extent of their property, the natural objects of their bounty, and the effect of the testamentary act
writing_requirement: Must be in writing or typewritten. Holographic wills (entirely in testator's handwriting) are also valid (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112)

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Some person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be competent witnesses. May appear in person or via audio-video communication technology
witness_requirements.presence_rules: Two competent witnesses must see the testator sign the will. Remote witnessing is permitted via audio-video communication technology where witnesses can see and hear the testator in real time (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: No subscribing witness may derive any benefit from the will unless there are at least two other disinterested and competent witnesses. If without a will the interested witness would be entitled to a share, they may still receive up to that intestate share amount (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity. Required for self-proving affidavits

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: The testator and witnesses must sign an affidavit before an officer authorized to administer oaths, evidenced by the officer's certificate under official seal. Can be done simultaneously with execution or at any subsequent date (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-114)

electronic_wills.notes: Wyoming does not currently allow electronic wills. However, Wyoming does allow witnesses to appear remotely via audio-video communication technology and to sign the will electronically. Notaries may also appear remotely

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if entirely in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required for a holographic will (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-113)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Wyoming

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) with intent to revoke
Another person may destroy the will at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Wyoming is a separate property state. Wyoming's will statute allows remote witnessing via audio-video communication technology (Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112), making it one of the more flexible states for will execution without requiring a full electronic wills act. Interested witness provisions are stricter than many UPC states, requiring two additional disinterested witnesses if a witness is also a beneficiary
statute_citations: Wyo. Stat. 2-6-101 (Who may make a will)
Wyo. Stat. 2-6-112 (Will to be in writing; witnesses; signature; subscribing witness not to benefit)
Wyo. Stat. 2-6-117 (Revocation by writing or by act)
